blocks.html:
The Message Batches API takes a pile of independent requests, processes them in the background, and returns results when done. Two numbers define it: roughly 50% off on-demand pricing, and up to 24 hours to complete. Many batches finish sooner — but you design against the ceiling, never the average.

The fit test is about time, not money: batch works for high-volume, delay-tolerant, independent requests. It fails for interactive users, short-deadline alerts, and chains where each step needs the previous result. Choosing batch "because it's half price" without the latency analysis is the named pitfall — a discount on results that arrive too late is a 100% loss.

One mechanical habit: results come back in arbitrary order, so every request carries a custom_id and your application joins results by it — never by position. Use stable unique IDs (the source record's primary key) so re-running failures stays unambiguous.

Documents arrive all day; results must be usable within 30 hours; batches take up to 24; post-processing takes 4. The worst-case document arrives one second after a submission and waits the whole interval for the next one. So: max cadence = 30 − 24 − 4 = 2 hours. The arithmetic is always deadline minus batch ceiling minus processing buffer — and the slowest record sets the constraint. Corollary: a once-a-day submission is only safe when the SLA is at least 48 hours (24h wait + 24h window).

Failure handling is surgical, never wholesale. When 3% of a batch fails: chunk the context_length_exceeded inputs and merge partials; resubmit validation failures with error feedback; refine prompts for schema issues; resubmit expired/canceled custom_ids — and never rerun the successful 97%. Operationally: results are JSONL (stream them incrementally), validate your request shape on the standard API first (one malformed request won't fail the batch — it becomes a per-request error you must reconcile), and expect platform size limits to force multiple batches. The batch discount and prompt caching stack — batched requests support caching — though cache hits inside an async batch are best-effort since requests may process far apart. Neither lever fixes the other's limits.

items: ~50% off, up to 24 hours — design SLAs on the worst case.
Fit is decided by latency tolerance, not by the discount.
Join results by custom_id, never position; keep IDs stable and unique.
Cadence formula: deadline − 24h batch ceiling − processing buffer.
Resubmit only failures, handled by failure type.
